g.data-narration: The CAP Theorem is a fundamental principle in distributed computing that defines essential trade-offs between data guarantees.
CAP Theorem states that distributed databases can provide only two of three guarantees: Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance.
g.text: A distributed database system can provide only

g.data-narration: Understanding CAP Theorem is crucial for designing databases that balance consistency, availability, and fault tolerance effectively.
g.text: • Essential for cloud computing and distributed database design
• Explains trade-offs between NoSQL and traditional databases
• Guides architecture decisions for scalable systems

g.data-narration: Consistency ensures all nodes see the same data simultaneously; every read returns the most recent write or an error.
Availability guarantees every request receives a response, ensuring the system remains operational even when some nodes fail.
Partition Tolerance allows the system to continue operating despite network failures between nodes in distributed systems.
The CAP triangle visually shows the trade-off: during network partitions, you must choose between Consistency or Availability.
Distributed systems fall into three categories: CA (consistency+availability), CP (consistency+partition tolerance), or AP (availability+partition tolerance).
Popular databases exemplify CAP choices: Cassandra is AP, MongoDB defaults to CP, and DynamoDB prioritizes availability.
PACELC extends CAP by considering latency-consistency trade-offs even when no network partition occurs in distributed systems.
g.text: When a partition (P) occurs → choose Availability (A) or Consistency (C)
Else (E), when no partition → choose Latency (L) or Consistency (C)
Addresses latency trade-offs in normal operation

g.data-narration: Eventual consistency is a weaker model where all replicas become consistent over time if no new updates are made to the data.
g.text: Acceptable for: social media, likes, comments, caches

g.data-narration: Banking systems require strong consistency (CP) because financial transactions demand atomic updates across all nodes.
g.text: Scenario: Transfer Rs. 10,000 from Account X to Account Y during network partition
Transaction halted until partition resolves → Strong consistency

g.data-narration: MongoDB allows configurable CAP preferences through write and read concerns for different application requirements.
Key takeaways: network partitions are inevitable, choose CP or AP during partitions, CA systems don't truly exist in distributed environments.
g.text: Network partitions are inevitable → P is mandatory
During partition: choose C or A, not both
CA systems don't exist in practice

g.data-narration: Remember: CAP acronym, real-world examples (Cassandra=AP, MongoDB=CP), application drives the choice, PACELC extends CAP.
g.text: Application requirements should drive CAP trade-off decisions
